source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
title: Ensure the default security group of every VPC restricts all traffic

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure the default security group of every VPC restricts all traffic
input_row.description: A VPC comes with a default security group whose initial settings deny all inbound traffic, allow all outbound traffic, and allow all traffic between instances assigned to the security group. If a security group is not specified when an instance is launched, it is automatically assigned to this default security group. Security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to AWS resources. It is recommended that the default security group restrict all traffic, both inbound and outbound. The default VPC in every region should have its default security group updated to comply with the following: • No inbound rules. • No outbound rules. Any newly created VPCs will automatically contain a default security group that will need remediation to comply with this recommendation. Note: When implementing this recommendation, VPC flow logging is invaluable in determining the least privilege port access required by systems to work properly, as it can log all packet acceptances and rejections occurring under the current security groups. This dramatically reduces the primary barrier to least privilege engineering by discovering the minimum ports required by systems in the environment. Even if the VPC flow logging recommendation in this benchmark is not adopted as a permanent security measure, it should be used during any period of discovery and engineering for least privileged security groups.
input_row.rationale: Configuring all VPC default security groups to restrict all traffic will encourage the development of least privilege security groups and promote the mindful placement of AWS resources into security groups, which will, in turn, reduce the exposure of those resources. Impact: Implementing this recommendation in an existing VPC that contains operating resources requires extremely careful migration planning, as the default security groups are likely enabling many ports that are unknown. Enabling VPC flow logging (for accepted connections) in an existing environment that is known to be breach-free will reveal the current pattern of ports being used for each instance to communicate successfully. The migration process should include: • Analyzing VPC flow logs to understand current traffic patterns. • Creating least privilege security groups based on the analyzed data. • Testing the new security group rules in a staging environment before applying them to production.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if the account is configured as prescribed: Security Group State 1. Login to the AWS VPC Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/home. 2. Repeat the following steps for all VPCs, including the default VPC in each AWS region: 3. In the left pane, click Security Groups. 4. For each default security group, perform the following: o Select the default security group. o Click the Inbound Rules tab and ensure no rules exist. o Click the Outbound Rules tab and ensure no rules exist. Security Group Members 1. Login to the AWS VPC Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/home. 2. Repeat the following steps for all default groups in all VPCs, including the default VPC in each AWS region: 3. In the left pane, click Security Groups. 4. Copy the ID of the default security group. 5. Change to the EC2 Management Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/v2/home. 6. In the filter column type Security Group ID : <security-group-id-from- step-4>.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to implement the prescribed state: Security Group Members 1. Identify AWS resources that exist within the default security group. 2. Create a set of least-privilege security groups for those resources. 3. Place the resources in those security groups, removing the resources noted in step 1 from the default security group. Security Group State 1. Login to the AWS VPC Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/home. 2. Repeat the following steps for all VPCs, including the default VPC in each AWS region: 3. In the left pane, click Security Groups. 4. For each default security group, perform the following: o Select the default security group. o Click the Inbound Rules tab. o Remove any inbound rules. o Click the Outbound Rules tab. o Remove any Outbound rules. Recommended IAM groups allow you to edit the "name" field. After remediating default group rules for all VPCs in all regions, edit this field to add text similar to "DO NOT USE. DO NOT ADD RULES." References: 1. CCE-79201-0 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network- security.html 3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-security- groups.html#default-security-group
input_row.impact: Implementing this recommendation in an existing VPC that contains operating resources requires extremely careful migration planning, as the default security groups are likely enabling many ports that are unknown. Enabling VPC flow logging (for accepted connections) in an existing environment that is known to be breach-free will reveal the current pattern of ports being used for each instance to communicate successfully. The migration process should include: • Analyzing VPC flow logs to understand current traffic patterns. • Creating least privilege security groups based on the analyzed data. • Testing the new security group rules in a staging environment before applying them to production.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control pertains to EC2 security groups, specifically verifying the configuration of default security groups within each VPC. This task can be fully accomplished using AWS APIs, as security group configurations are standard properties exposed via the APIs. Techniques such as querying the state of inbound and outbound rules using the AWS CLI or boto3 SDK are well-suited to automate this check, avoiding the need for manual intervention.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_ec2_default_security_group_all_traffic_restricted

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS API calls or boto3 SDK methods to use:**
  - Use `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()` to retrieve details about all security groups. Filter by those with `GroupName` set to "default."
  - For each default security group, inspect both `IpPermissions` (inbound rules) and `IpPermissionsEgress` (outbound rules) to ensure that they are empty.

- **AWS CLI command (if applicable):**
  - Use `aws ec2 describe-security-groups --filters "Name=group-name,Values=default"` to obtain default security group details.
  - Check that `IpPermissions` and `IpPermissionsEgress` are empty for compliance.

- **Specific resources to enumerate:**
  - Enumerate through each AWS region to ensure that the default security groups for all VPCs (including the default VPC) are audited.

- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - Ensure that `IpPermissions` and `IpPermissionsEgress` for each default security group are empty (i.e., no traffic is allowed inbound or outbound).

- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - **Pass:** The default security group has neither inbound nor outbound rules configured (`IpPermissions` and `IpPermissionsEgress` are empty).
  - **Fail:** Either inbound or outbound rules are present in the default security group (`IpPermissions` or `IpPermissionsEgress` is not empty).

This automated approach leverages AWS's APIs to systematically verify compliance across all VPCs in multiple regions, ensuring that default security groups do not allow any traffic, thereby supporting least privilege access principles.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as "Automated" is correct. The task of verifying the configuration of default security groups can be effectively automated using AWS APIs, boto3, or the AWS CLI. These tools provide the necessary capabilities to retrieve and inspect security group configurations across all VPCs and regions.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- The automation details are technically accurate. The use of `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups()` and the corresponding AWS CLI command `aws ec2 describe-security-groups --filters "Name=group-name,Values=default"` are valid and appropriate for retrieving security group details.
- The inspection of `IpPermissions` and `IpPermissionsEgress` to ensure they are empty is a correct approach to verify compliance with the control.
- The program name, `aws_ec2_default_security_group_all_traffic_restricted`, is descriptive and clear, although it could be slightly refined for clarity and brevity.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name Suggestion:** Consider renaming the program to `aws_ec2_default_sg_no_traffic` for brevity while maintaining clarity.
- **Missing Considerations:** The assessment should mention the need to handle potential rate limits when querying multiple regions, especially in large AWS environments. Implementing pagination with `describe_security_groups()` is also important to handle cases where the number of security groups exceeds the API's default response limit.
- **Alternative AWS API Methods:** Consider using AWS Config rules such as `vpc-default-security-group-closed` for ongoing compliance monitoring, which can complement the automated checks.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded and technically sound. The use of AWS APIs and CLI for automation is appropriate, and the steps outlined are comprehensive for the task.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Keep as-is

- The current assessment is accurate and well-structured. The only suggested modification is a slight refinement of the program name for clarity. The approach is robust and aligns with AWS security best practices.
